content.full_text:
DARRYL R. WOLD
ATTORNEY AT LAW
315 AIRWAY AVENUE, SUITE M-1
COSTA MESA, CALIFORNIA 92626
(714) 641-4942    FAX (714) 546-1003

September 6, 1990


Deanne Stone, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J. Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95804-0814

     Re:  Interpretation of §85802(f) Discussed on July 24, 1990

Dear Ms. Stone:

     I recently asked you by telephone whether it would be
permissible for an incumbent officeholder to use campaign funds
to pay for the cost of an annual luncheon attended by certain of
his agency's employees, and by the spouse or other guest of each
of those employees, where the cost of each lunch was less than
$50, and the cost of lunch for the employee and a spouse or other
guest, together, would be less than $100.

     You replied by telephone on July 24, 1990, that the test
would be whether the expenditure was "directly related" to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose as required by
Government Code §85802(f). The only provision in §85802(f)
specifically describing expenditures that are "directly related"
to a permitted purpose refers to gifts of less than $100 in a
calendar year made to "an employee or a campaign committee
worker, or to an employee of the elected officer's agency." You
said that since there is no Commission regulation or previous
written advice providing further guidance, your verbal advice,
based on the literal language of §85802(f), would have to be that
use of campaign funds to pay the cost of the lunch for the
employee would be permissible, but that payment for the cost of
the lunch for a spouse or other guest of the employee is not
defined as "directly related" to a permitted purpose so would not
be permissible.

     On behalf of my client, Brad Gates, the incumbent Sheriff of
Orange County, I am asking for written advice on this question.
I am authorized to make this request on behalf of this client.

Deanne Stone, Esq.
September 6, 1990
Page Two


I am also urging very strongly that, in providing written
advice, the Commission reconsider the advice that you gave me
verbally, based on the literal language of §85802(f) alone, and
that the Commission advise me that the cost of the lunch for the
spouse or other guest of an employee of an officeholder's agency
would be considered "directly related" to a permitted purpose
where the spouse or other guest was invited at the option of the
employee, attended with the employee, and the combined cost was
less than $100. I believe that this interpretation is the
appropriate interpretation of §85802(f), and that there are sound
reasons for this interpretation.

     Permitting an employee to invite a spouse or guest to attend
an event with that employee, at the employee's option, should be
considered a gift to the employee, and therefore be "directly
related" to a permitted purpose under the specific provision of
§85802(f)(2) permitting gifts to employees of an elected
officeholder's agency.  (By the same token, the total cost of
attendance by the employee and the employee's spouse or other
guest would of course have to be combined for purposes of the
"less than $100" limitation.)  It is clear from the circumstances
of this type of gift that the purpose of inviting the spouse or
other guest is for the benefit of the employee, and not for some
independent reason unrelated to the benefit to the employee.  The
analysis that the cost of attendance is a gift to the employee is
therefore much more compelling than the contrary analysis -- that
is, that the payment for the cost of attendance constitutes
separate gifts to the employee and to the employee's spouse or
guest.

     The contrary analysis -- that is, that a gift is deemed
given to each of the individuals who actually use it -- would
create an artificial and unnecessary distinction in the type of
gifts that could be given to an employee.  That analysis would,
for instance, prohibit gifts such as two tickets to a single
event (for an employee and the employee's spouse or other guest),
but permit a gift of one ticket to each of two events (which
could be used by the employee only).  Falling somewhere in
between might be a gift to an employee for the employee's home,
where a spouse might also enjoy it, or a gift such as a basket of
fruit and wine, clearly intended for the enjoyment of two
persons, even though technically it could be consumed in its
entirety by one self-indulgent individual.  (This analysis would,
of course, also apply to gifts to persons other than employees,
such as to an officeholder's political supporters.  The
officeholder could give one ticket to an event to a campaign
contributor, but not two, unless the contributor used it only for

Deanne Stone, Esq.
September 6, 1990
Page Three


a person who is also a contributor or for whom the gift would
have an independent "directly related" purpose.)  These
distinctions are artificial and totally unnecessary to serve the
reasonable purposes of §85802(f). The analysis that I urge the
Commission to adopt instead would avoid hairsplitting
interpretations in the future, where it might be questioned
whether a gift was intended for one or two persons. A gift to an
individual who is an employee or campaign committee worker, or an
employee of the elected officer's agency, should be considered a
gift to that individual even if it can be used or consumed only
by two persons.

     Lastly, the analysis that I am urging would be consistent
with California's community property law, which provides that a
gift to an individual is that individual's separate property
(Civil Code §§5107, 5108). A gift to an employee, therefore,
whether cash, a basket of fruit or wine, or two tickets to an
event, or lunch for the employee and the employee's spouse or
other guest, would be considered the separate property of that
employee, and therefore should be considered to be a gift to that
employee and not in part a gift to whoever else receives part of
the enjoyment of it at the invitation of the employee.

     I appreciate your consideration of this request. I think
that your experience may indicate that it is a problem that may
be encountered by a number of officeholders, so would justify the
Commission's close attention.

     If you have any questions, please let me know.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        DARRYL R. WOLD

DRW/km

STONE.LTR

sections.question_synthetic: The attorney requests written FPPC advice on whether campaign funds can be used to pay for an employee luncheon event where each employee's meal costs less than $50 and the combined cost for an employee plus spouse/guest is less than $100, seeking a broader interpretation of Government Code §85802(f) than previously given verbally.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from an attorney seeking written advice from FPPC. No formal FPPC response or conclusion is included. The document contains the legal question and detailed arguments supporting a requested interpretation, but FPPC has not yet issued their formal written advice.

embedding.summary: Attorney Darryl Mold requests written FPPC advice on behalf of Brad Gates, Orange County Sheriff, regarding the permissibility of using campaign funds to pay for annual employee luncheons that include spouses or guests of employees, where costs are under specified limits. The attorney argues that prior verbal advice was too restrictive and urges reconsideration to allow spouse/guest meal costs as gifts to the employee under §85802(f).
